coredata.dc:title: Deep ultraviolet emission photoluminescence and high luminescece efficiency of ferric passivated graphene quantum dots: Strong negative inductive effect of Fe
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

Yang, Siwei
Sun, Jing
He, Peng
Yuan, Ningyi

Xie, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: It is necessary to broaden the finite band gap of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and prepare deep ultraviolet emission GQDs with high luminescence efficiency. In this study, the deep ultraviolet emission ferric passivated GQDs (Fe-GQDs) are successfully prepared. Due to the strong negative inductive effect of Fe, the band gap of the Fe-GQDs was broadened, and the photoluminescence emission wavelength is 275nm for an excitation at 224nm with a very high fluorescent quantum yield (φ =0.63). This is the shortest emission wavelength reported for solution-based inorganic and organic quantum dots. The high efficient ultraviolet emission, as well as the good stability and low in vitro cytotoxicity, may facilitate their applications to carbon-based light-emitting devices and lasers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Quantum dots
Negative inductive effect
Deep ultraviolet

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and corresponding size distribution histogram of Fe-GQDs, the black curve is the Gaussian fitting curve. Inset: HRTEM image of a single Fe-GQDs particle. (b) AFM topography image and the height profile analysis along the line shown in the image of Fe-GQDs on a mica substrate. XPS spectra of Fe-GQDs.

(a) Normalized PLE and PL spectra of GOQDs and Fe-GQDs. (b) A brief summary of λ em and φ of carbon based materials, fluorescent dye and rare earth compounds [33–56]. (c) The UV–vis absorption spectra of undoped GQDs (black curve) and Fe-GQDs (red curve) (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

PL spectra of (a) GOQDs and (b) Fe-GQDs with different excitation wavelengths. (c) PL spectra of GOQDs and Fe-GQDs with different Fe contents. (d) λ em (black curve) and φ (red curve) of Fe-GQDs with different N content. (e) The schematic diagrams of energy level of GOQDs and Fe-GQDs with different Fe contents (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

head.ce:title: Deep ultraviolet emission photoluminescence and high luminescece efficiency of ferric passivated graphene quantum dots: Strong negative inductive effect of Fe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Photovolatic Science and Engineering, Jiangsu Province Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Photovoltaic Science and Technology, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, Jiangsu, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is necessary to broaden the finite band gap of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and prepare deep ultraviolet emission GQDs with high luminescence efficiency. In this study, the deep ultraviolet emission ferric passivated GQDs (Fe-GQDs) are successfully prepared. Due to the strong negative inductive effect of Fe, the band gap of the Fe-GQDs was broadened, and the photoluminescence emission wavelength is 275nm for an excitation at 224nm with a very high fluorescent quantum yield (φ =0.63). This is the shortest emission wavelength reported for solution-based inorganic and organic quantum dots. The high efficient ultraviolet emission, as well as the good stability and low in vitro cytotoxicity, may facilitate their applications to carbon-based light-emitting devices and lasers.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanometer-sized fragment of graphene that exibit unique properties, such as a high photostability, a low in vitro cytotoxicity and chemical inertness, which makes them interesting candidates for a wide range of new applications [1–5]. It is well known that the semimetallic graphene can be converted into a semiconducting form with a finite band gap (E g) via the electron confinement, which is the key factor for its photoluminescence (PL) [6,7]. Having a controllable nanometer-size, GQDs can effecitively produce a tunable E g and tunable emission effectively. The PL emission wavelength (λ em) can be modulated from ultraviolet to infrared wavelengths with a decrease in E g. In addition to control of size, doping has been widely employed for control and modification of the E g and increasing the luminescence efficiency (quantum yield, φ) of the GQDs. Currently, most of the reported GQDs exhibit a value of λ em in the range of 400–600nm in the visible-light region with low φ. Li et al. [8] synthesized sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots with stable blue–green fluorescence by the one-step electrolysis of graphite, demonstrating λ em =480nm and φ =0.106. Sun et al. [9] reported highly blue-luminescent nitrogen-doped GQDs with the strongest emission peak at an excitation of 436nm and φ =0.164 through a hydrothermal treatment.

Deep ultraviolet emission materials with high φ have considerable application value to high energy lasers [10] or light-emitting diodes [11]. So far, most deep ultraviolet emission materials are based on rare earth compounds and organic fluorescent dyes [12]. However, the high cost of rare earth compounds and the low stability of organic fluorescent dyes limit their respective development space [13]. On the other hand, the shorter wavelengths in the deep ultraviolet spectrum can be used to improve the resolution of fluorescent bioimaging [14–16]. However, the rare earth compounds and organic fluorescent dyes can hardly be applied in such applications owing to their in vitro cytotoxicity [17,18]. As a new PL material with attractive prospects, GQDs with deep ultraviolet emission (λ em <300nm) and high φ have not been reported. Considering the high photostability, low in vitro cytotoxicity and low cost of GQDs, it is necessary to broaden the E g of the GQDs and prepare new GQDs based deep ultraviolet emission materials with high φ.

Here, deep ultraviolet emission photoluminescent passivated GQDs (Fe-GQDs) were prepared through a hydrothermal reaction. λ em of the Fe-GQDs is 275nm for an excitation wavelength (λ ex) of 224nm. This is the shortest emission wavelength reported from solution-based inorganic and organic quantum dots. The Fe-GQDs have excellent photostability and high φ (0.63). The surface passivation and strong negative inductive effect of Fe lead to deep ultraviolet emission photoluminescence and high φ of Fe-GQDs. In addition, we also investigated the in vitro cytotoxicity of the Fe-GQDs in consideration of the application to deep ultraviolet emission bioimaging. The low toxicity of the Fe-GQDs indicates that they have wide applicability to practical applications.

Fig. 1 a shows a low magnification TEM image and the corresponding size distribution histogram of the Fe-GQDs. A Gaussian distribution of homogeneous dots with a lateral size distribution of 1–8nm and an average diameter of 3.7nm is observed. The full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM) of the distribution is 2.4nm, demonstrating a narrow distribution of the Fe-GQDs (doubly confirmed using atomic force microscopy observations (AFM), Fig. 1b). The size distribution exhibits no obvious change after doping (Figs. S1 and 2). The insert in Fig. 1a shows a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the 0.21nm lattice fringes of the Fe-GQDs which can be attributed to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon [19]. AFM reveals typical topographic height show trapezoidal morphologies with a height of 0.5–1.0nm. This indicates most Fe-GQDs consist of 1–3 graphene layers.

The XPS and FT-IR spectra are analyzed to verify the structure and formation of the as-prepared Fe-GQDs. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S3) for the Fe-GQDs shows a predominant narrow graphitic C 1s peak at ca. 284eV, along with an O 1s peak at ca. 532eV, and an Fe 2p peak at ca. 710eV [20]. The elemental composition of the Fe-GQDs is summarized in Table S1. The dosage concentration (Fe content) is 5.7at.%. The oxygen content of the Fe-GQDs is 21.6at.%, which is lower than precursor (graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), Table S1). This indicates the removal of oxygen-containing groups during doping. Fig. 1c shows the C 1s XPS spectrum of the Fe-GQDs. The chemical environments that are centred at 284.5eV (CC/CC), 285.5eV (COH/COC) and 288.6eV (CO) can be observed [20–22]. The percentages of the CC/CC, COH/COC and CO bonding mode are 43.05%, 39.07% and 17.88%, respectively. A further analysis reveals the change in the oxygen-containing groups during doping. The well-fitted O 1s (Fig. S5) spectrum can be divided into three different peaks, which are assigned to CO at 531.6eV, COH/COC at 533.1eV and FeO at 532eV [23]. The percentages of the CO, COH/COC and FeO bonding modes are 17.3%, 27.1% and 55.6%, respectively. However, the O 1s (Fig. S6) spectrum of the precursor can be divided into two peaks which are assigned to CO (531.6eV) and COH/COC (533.1eV). The percentages of the CO and COH/COC bonding mode are 39.62% and 60.38%, respectively (doubly confirmed using FT-IR spectrum, Fig. S8). There is no obvious change in the contents of CO and COH/COC before and after doping. Moreover, the new FeO bond which is not observed in the O 1s spectrum of the precursor (Fig. S7) illustrates that coordination bonding is generated between the precursor of iron benzoate and carboxyl [24–26]. Finally, as shown in Fig. 1d, the Fe 2p spectra are split into two parts owing to spin–orbit coupling, namely Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, with an intensity ratio of approximately 2/1. Each part consists of a main peak and a “shake-up” satellite [27,28]. The peaks located at 711.0eV and 724.6eV correspond to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. The satellite peak obtained at 718.8eV is clearly distinguishable and does not overlap with the Fe 2p3/2 or Fe 2p1/2 peaks. In addition, there appears to be another satellite peak at 729.5eV, which may be a satellite peak for Fe 2p1/2 [29]. Thus, the Fe(III) coordinated with the carboxylic acid groups of GQDs after hydrothermal process (Fig. 1e).

The blue-shifted PL excitation (PLE) and PL spectra are shown in Fig. 2 a. λ ex and λ em of the Fe-GQDs are at 224 and 275nm, respectively. The Stokes shift and FWHM of the Fe-GQDs are 0.20eV (41nm) and 0.24eV (50nm), respectively. The low value of Δν St indicates that the Fe-GQDs have a weak self-absorption effect and low energy loss [30]. φ of Fe-GQDs is 0.63, which is higher than most previous reports (Fig. 2b). The ultrahigh φ of the Fe-GQDs can be due to the surface-passivation of Fe [31].

Presently observed φ of the Fe-GQDs is comparable with those of traditionally prepared and passivated silicon nanocrystals [32]. The λ em of Fe-GQDs is the shortest emission wavelength reported for solution-based inorganic and organic quantum dots. Moreover, it is shorter than all other carbon based materials and most fluorescent dye or rare earth compounds (Fig. 2b). The PL decay of the Fe-GQDs is measured by a time-correlated single photon counting technique and fitted well with a biexponential decay function as shown in Fig. S10. The lifetime (τ) is calculated as follow formula:
τ =
(
α
1
τ
1
2
+
α
2
τ
2
2
)
(
α
1
τ
1
+
α
2
τ
2
)
where τ 1 and τ 2 represent long and short decay, respectively. α 1 and α 2 represent the fractional weights of τ 1 and τ 2, respectively. The τ is dominated by a long decay component of 6.4ns (83%) plus a small contribution from the short decay of 2.1ns (17%) and the weighted-average lifetime is approximately 5.7ns. The radiative rate κ r can be obtained with φ and τ by κ r = φ/τ and is 1.1×108 s−1 for Fe-GQDs [57].

Fig. 2c shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the precursor (undoped GQDs, black curve) and Fe-GQDs (red curve). The absorption spectrum of the precursor exhibits two absorption peaks at 365 and 255nm. The peak at 255nm, which is consistent with the π–π* transition of the sp2 domains (aromatic nucleus), results in nearly no observed PL emission. Another significant peak at approximately 365nm due to the n–π* transition, namely, the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states, leads to a strong emission [58]. The absorption spectrum of the Fe-GQDs, a UV–vis absorption band is observed with a strong shoulder at 350nm, which is blue-shifted by approximately 15nm with respect to that of the undoped GQDs.

This absorption peak can be assigned to the π transition of the aromatic sp2 domains. The absorption peak of the π–π* transition model is also blue-shifted by approximately 10nm after doping progress. These blue-shifted absorption peaks can be caused by the strong negative inductive effect of Fe in the Fe-GQDs. The Fe-GQDs also exhibited excellent stability after continuous excitation under visible light for more than 120 days (Fig. S11). Moreover, no obvious change in the PL intensity is observed under illumination by a 150W Xe lamp with a centre wavelength of 320nm for 60h. Moreover, extremely small change in the PL intensity can be observed for ion strength (0.2–1.4molL−1, Fig S12) and pH (4–12, Fig. S13). This indicates that the Fe-GQDs are stable in a soft environment. The Fe-GQDs also have a low cytotoxicity and can be used in practical applications (Fig. S14).

Detailed PL experiments are carried out to study the mechanism of high φ and deep ultraviolet PL. The GOQDs have excitation-dependent PL emission (Fig. 3 a), which exhibits an emission peak at 401nm at 478nm, with a maxima λ em shift of 75nm when the λ ex increases from 280 to 425nm. The excitation-dependent PL emission is due to the co-existence of GOQDs with diversified surface oxygen groups. On the other hand, the emission peak does not shift much with a maxima red shift of 15nm when the λ ex of the Fe-GQDs increases from 210 to 240nm (Fig. 3b). This indicates the unified chromophores of Fe-GQDs which result in the high φ of Fe-GQDs [17,20].

To verify the negative inductive effect of Fe on PL properties, the Fe-GQDs with different Fe contents and same oxygen content were prepared. Fe-GQDs with different Fe contents show significant spectral shift (Fig. 3c and d). The λem of Fe-GQDs with lower Fe content (Fe-GQDs-1, Fe contents: 3.1at.%, Fig. S15) is red shifted to the 312nm. What is more, the PL spectrum further red shifted with decreased Fe contents (Fe-GQD-2 and Fe-GQD-3, Fe contents: 1.7 and 0.8at.%, Figs. S16 and 17). This indicates the strong negative inductive effect of Fe is the key factor for the deep ultraviolet PL. Moreover, the φ of Fe-GQDs is 0.63, 0.47, 0.30 and 0.18 when the Fe content is 5.6, 3.1, 1.7 and 0.8, respectively (Fig. 3d). The decreasing of quantum yield can be due to the week passivation when the Fe content is low.

A further experiment confirms the strong negative inductive effect of Fe. The Fe-GQDs are refluxed in 1M HCl for 48h to remove the Fe. λ ex is red shifted from 275 to 440nm (Fig. S18), indicating the Fe effectively broadens E g of the GQDs, which can be due to the strong negative inductive effect of Fe. Moreover, φ is 0.13 after the reflux progress. This indicates that the passivating action of Fe can effectively improve φ of the GQDs [60].

Based on above results, the change of E g of GQDs is shown in Fig. 3d. As for the Fe-GQDs, the E g is much higher than that for the GOQDs. The strong negative inductive effect of Fe decreases the π electric cloud distribution in the Fe-GQDs by the coordination bond between Fe and carboxyl. The absorption of UV photons (λ =224nm) by a localized π electron in the double bond produces an electron–hole pair (exciton) after an electronic transition (π–π*, v2–c2) [59,60]. The exciton may emit near-ultraviolet light (c2–v2) through radiative recombination after vibrational relaxation [61]. The excited electron may also undergo an interband transition from a higher conduction band to a lower conduction band (e.g., c2–c1) which leads to peak spreading in the PL spectrum. Moreover, the absorption spectrum indicates that the n–π* transition (c1–v1) is a nonradiative level [62,63].

In summary, the near-ultraviolet photoluminescent Fe-GQDs are prepared via a hydrothermal reaction. The surface passivation and strong negative inductive effect of Fe lead to a high luminescence efficiency and near-ultraviolet photoluminescence. The Fe-GQDs exhibit the shortest emission wavelength reported from solution-based inorganic and organic quantum dots. The high luminescence efficiency (φ =0.63) is also remarkable. The mechanism of this high efficient near-ultraviolet emission is proposed and experimentally confirmed. Moreover, the Fe-GQDs also exhibit good stability and low in vitro cytotoxicity. This work is helpful for preparing new GQDs-based near-ultraviolet emission materials with high luminescence efficiency, and may facilitate their applications to carbon-based light-emitting devices and lasers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from the National Science and Technology Major Project (Grant no. 2011ZX02707), the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant nos. KGZD-EW-303 and XDA02040000), and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions on Renewable Energy Materials Science and Engineering.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.synthmet.2015.08.025.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
